role_summary: President of the University of Maryland, Baltimore County (1992-2022); founder of UMBC's Meyerhoff Scholars Program; arrested at twelve in the 1963 Birmingham Children's Crusade.
ai_reading: Hrabowski sits at -0.81 on the Tactical Pragmatism vs Strategic Vision axis, far toward Tactical Pragmatism, the most-tactical reading in the catalog's audited subset so far. This is the AI-vs-culture mismatch on his page: a thirty-year university president whose institutional posture and published books (The Empowered University, 2019; The Resilient University, 2024) are explicitly long-arc strategic-vision work, lands on the opposite pole of the axis in his oral history. The embedding may be catching the specificity of the Birmingham 1963 narrative (the Children's Crusade arrest at age twelve dominates his interview's most-cited passages) and reading those concrete-action memories as tactical rather than as the strategic-pedagogical framing his administrative career carries. His top semantic neighbors are the Abernathy family (entry 28, count 99), Jennifer Lawson (entry 59), Eddie Holloway (entry 33), Harold K. Brown (entry 55), and Mildred Pitts Walter (entry 91), a Birmingham-and-education cluster that confirms the Birmingham anchor over the Baltimore presidency in the vector signature.
biographical_paragraph: Hrabowski was twelve in May 1963 when he was arrested in Birmingham during what later was called the Children's Crusade, the mass-protest action that drew school-age Black children into the city's segregation campaign; his arrest is one of the most-cited single scenes in his interview record [src: 2]. He went on to a forty-year career in higher education that culminated in three decades (1992-2022) as president of the University of Maryland, Baltimore County, where he founded and chaired the Meyerhoff Scholars Program supporting Black students into STEM doctorates [src: 3]. He has published with Johns Hopkins University Press: The Empowered University (2019) and The Resilient University (2024) [src: 3]. His Library of Congress oral history is entry 45 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 1].
